_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against 2014 APTA Heel Pain CPG (Martin), 2023 APTA CPG update, Riddle 2003 (BMI/standing/dorsiflexion), Cotchett 2014 trigger-point dry needling RCT, DiGiovanni 2003/2006 plantar-fascia-specific stretching, Babatunde 2019 ESWT network meta-analysis, ACFAS clinical consensus, Botek 2024 gastrocnemius recession review, Hurley 2020 PRP meta-analysis. Drug names and doses checked against UpToDate Lexicomp 2025.

h1Title: Plantar Fasciitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Plantar fasciitis is degeneration and microtearing of the plantar fascia at its calcaneal origin, and it is the single most common cause of heel pain in adults — about 1 in 10 people develop it during their lifetime, and US clinicians see roughly 2 million cases each year. The hallmark is a sharp, stabbing pain under the inside of the heel during the first few steps in the morning, easing after walking and returning after long periods of standing. Most cases follow a sudden rise in activity, weight gain, or a switch to less supportive footwear, and the underlying biology is non-inflammatory tendinosis rather than classic inflammation. With targeted stretching, supportive shoe choice, and time, around 90% of patients recover within 6-12 months without surgery, though refractory cases benefit from shockwave therapy or, rarely, gastrocnemius recession.

keyStats.prevalence: Lifetime risk roughly 10% in US adults; accounts for ~1% of all primary care musculoskeletal visits
keyStats.demographics: Peak age 40-60; women slightly more affected than men; obesity and prolonged standing occupations are the strongest risk factors
keyStats.avgAge: Most patients present between ages 40 and 60; runners present earlier (ages 25-45)
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 2 million US clinical encounters yearly; one of the top three reasons for adult foot specialist visits worldwide

definition: Plantar fasciitis (ICD-10: M72.2, plantar fascial fibromatosis) is a degenerative enthesopathy of the plantar aponeurosis — the thick band of collagen that runs from the medial calcaneal tuberosity along the sole of the foot to the base of the toes and supports the medial longitudinal arch. The condition was historically labeled an inflammatory tendinitis, but biopsy and histology studies from the 1990s onward consistently show collagen disorganization, mucoid degeneration, fibroblast proliferation, and microtears with little or no acute inflammatory infiltrate. The current preferred term in many sports medicine circles is plantar fasciopathy or plantar heel pain syndrome, though plantar fasciitis remains the dominant clinical and search term. Pathology centers on the proximal insertion at the medial calcaneal tubercle, where repetitive tensile load exceeds the tissue's capacity to repair. The condition is managed across podiatry, orthopedics, sports medicine, and physical therapy, with most cases treated outside the operating room. It sits inside a broader spectrum of plantar heel pain that includes calcaneal stress fracture, tarsal tunnel syndrome, fat pad atrophy, and Baxter's nerve entrapment, all of which can mimic it.

typesClassification.description: Recent onset, often after a clear trigger such as a marathon training block, a new job on hard floors, or rapid weight gain. Responds best to early load reduction, stretching, and supportive footwear.

typesClassification.type: Subacute plantar fasciitis (6 weeks to 6 months)
typesClassification.description: Symptoms persist past initial healing windows. Heel-cord and fascia-specific stretching, manual therapy, and orthoses become central. Imaging is reasonable if pain is not improving by month 3.
Pain persists despite 6+ months of conservative care. Extracorporeal shockwave therapy, ultrasound-guided needling, or surgical referral enter the picture. Around 10% of patients reach this stage.

Sudden tearing of the fascia, often during sprinting, jumping, or after a corticosteroid injection. Presents with a popping sensation, immediate bruising, and inability to bear weight on the forefoot.

primarySymptoms: Sharp, stabbing heel pain with the first few steps after getting out of bed in the morning or after sitting for a long period, easing within 5-15 minutes of walking — the most specific feature of the condition.
Tenderness on direct palpation of the medial calcaneal tubercle on the inner side of the heel, often reproducible with a thumb pressed firmly into the spot where the fascia inserts.
Pain that returns or worsens after extended standing, walking on hard floors, or at the end of a long workday, particularly in barefoot or unsupportive shoes.
A burning, aching, or tight sensation along the arch that radiates forward from the heel, worsening with toe-walking, climbing stairs, or pushing off during running.
Stiffness in the calf and Achilles tendon, with reduced ankle dorsiflexion that is typically worse on the affected side and contributes to the cycle of overload.
Pain provoked by passive dorsiflexion of the great toe (the windlass test), which stretches the fascia and reproduces the patient's typical heel pain.
An antalgic gait pattern, with the patient shifting weight onto the lateral border of the foot or toe-walking to avoid loading the heel.
Symptoms usually unilateral at onset but bilateral in 20-30% of patients, especially those with obesity, occupational standing, or systemic enthesopathy such as spondyloarthritis.
earlyWarningSigns: A mild morning twinge under the heel that fades within a minute of walking, occurring on the first 1-2 days after a long run, hike, or shift on your feet
Calf tightness or reduced ankle dorsiflexion noticed during stretching or yoga, often months before heel pain begins
A new ache under the arch after switching to flatter or less cushioned footwear, including barefoot shoes, ballet flats, or worn-out running shoes
Tenderness when pressing the inner aspect of the heel with a thumb, even before pain develops with walking
Sudden weight gain of more than 5-10% body weight or a recent pregnancy, both of which load the fascia disproportionately
emergencySigns: Sudden popping or tearing sensation under the foot during running, jumping, or after a steroid injection, followed by immediate severe pain and inability to push off — suspect plantar fascia rupture
Heel pain accompanied by numbness, tingling, or burning radiating into the sole or toes — consider tarsal tunnel syndrome or Baxter's nerve entrapment rather than fasciitis
Heel pain with redness, warmth, swelling, and fever — exclude calcaneal osteomyelitis or septic arthritis, especially in people with diabetes or recent foot wound
Diffuse heel pain that is severe at rest and worsens with any weight-bearing in a runner with recent training spike — calcaneal stress fracture must be ruled out with imaging

causes.cause: Repetitive tensile overload of the fascia
causes.description: The plantar fascia stretches under tension every time the foot bears weight. When daily load exceeds tissue tolerance over weeks to months — through more steps, more standing, or higher-impact activity — microtears accumulate at the calcaneal insertion faster than the tissue can repair, producing degenerative changes rather than classic inflammation.

causes.cause: Limited ankle dorsiflexion and tight gastrocnemius-soleus complex
causes.description: A tight calf forces the foot into early heel rise during gait, which loads the plantar fascia. Studies (Riddle 2003) show that dorsiflexion under 10 degrees raises plantar fasciitis risk roughly 23-fold. This is the single most important biomechanical contributor.

causes.cause: Obesity and elevated body mass index
causes.description: Mechanical loading is roughly proportional to body weight, and higher BMI is consistently associated with both incidence and persistence of plantar fasciitis. BMI above 30 raises risk approximately 5-6 fold (Riddle 2003) and predicts worse response to conservative care.

Jobs requiring prolonged standing or walking on hard surfaces — nurses, teachers, retail workers, factory operators, military recruits — markedly increase risk. Running, dancing, and court sports add high-impact tensile load to the fascia and are common precipitants in younger patients.

Both pes planus (flat foot, which overstretches the fascia) and pes cavus (high arch, which transmits load more focally to the heel) raise risk. Excessive pronation, leg-length discrepancy, and forefoot varus also contribute.

Switching to unsupportive or minimalist footwear, running in worn-out shoes (over 500 km), or changing to a harder surface (treadmill to pavement, carpet to concrete) frequently precipitates symptoms in someone with subclinical predisposition.

Bilateral plantar fasciitis in a younger patient should prompt screening for seronegative spondyloarthritis (ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis). The plantar fascia is a classic enthesis affected by these conditions.

riskFactors.description: Obesity is the most consistent epidemiologic risk factor across studies. BMI over 30 raises risk roughly 5-6 fold in non-athletes; weight loss measurably improves both incidence and treatment response.

Tight calves increase plantar fascia tensile load during gait. Riddle 2003 reported an odds ratio near 23 for plantar fasciitis when dorsiflexion was below 0 degrees relative to a more flexible reference group.

Standing or walking for more than 6 hours daily — common in nursing, teaching, retail, manufacturing, and military service — substantially raises incidence. Cushioned mats, supportive footwear, and rest breaks reduce risk.

riskFactors.factor: Sudden increase in running or jumping load
riskFactors.description: Adding mileage, hills, speed work, or starting a new running program too quickly is a leading trigger in athletes. Training spikes greater than 30% per week are particularly implicated.
Tissue elasticity, collagen turnover, and heel fat-pad cushioning all decline with age, making this the peak window for diagnosis. Runners often present earlier; sedentary patients later.
Women are diagnosed slightly more often than men, and pregnancy-related weight gain plus ligamentous laxity from relaxin contribute to a postpartum surge in cases.

riskFactors.factor: Pes planus or pes cavus foot type
riskFactors.description: Either extreme of arch height alters fascia loading. Flat feet overstretch the fascia; high arches concentrate load at the heel. Custom or prefabricated orthoses help both groups.

Shoes older than 500 km of running, completely flat sandals, ballet flats, and minimalist trainers without arch support are common precipitants. Switching to supportive footwear is often part of treatment.

HLA-B27-associated diseases (ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis) cause enthesitis at the calcaneal insertion. Bilateral disease in a young adult warrants rheumatology evaluation.
Diabetes is associated with thicker, less compliant plantar fascia and slower healing. Glycemic control modestly improves outcomes and reduces injection-related complications.

affectedDemographics: US clinical encounters: approximately 2 million per year, with lifetime prevalence near 10% of adults
Women are diagnosed slightly more often than men, with a 2:1 skew in some occupational cohorts (nursing, teaching)
Runners account for roughly 8-10% of all running-related injuries, making plantar fasciitis the second-most-common running injury after iliotibial band syndrome
Military recruits in basic training show incidence rates of 10-15% during initial weeks of high-volume marching and running
Bilateral disease occurs in 20-30% of patients overall but in over 50% of patients with spondyloarthritis-related enthesitis
diagnosisOverview: Plantar fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis. The history of sharp medial heel pain that is worst with the first morning steps and eases with walking, combined with point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle and a positive windlass test (pain reproduced by passive dorsiflexion of the great toe while the ankle is in neutral), is sufficient in most cases. Both the 2014 APTA Heel Pain clinical practice guideline and its 2023 update recommend against routine imaging for typical presentations. Imaging is reserved for atypical features — bilateral disease in a young adult, pain that does not improve at 3-6 months, sudden onset with a popping sensation, neurologic symptoms in the foot, or systemic features such as morning stiffness elsewhere. Diagnostic ultrasound is the imaging modality of choice and shows a thickened (greater than 4 mm) and hypoechoic plantar fascia at the calcaneal insertion, sometimes with intrasubstance tears or perifascial fluid. MRI is reserved for cases where the diagnosis is uncertain, where rupture or stress fracture is suspected, or before surgical planning. Plain radiographs frequently show an incidental calcaneal spur, but the spur is not the cause of pain — 15-20% of asymptomatic adults have one. Laboratory testing is unnecessary unless a systemic inflammatory cause is suspected, in which case HLA-B27, ESR, CRP, RF, and uric acid may be requested. The most important differential diagnoses to exclude are calcaneal stress fracture (positive squeeze test, pain at rest), tarsal tunnel syndrome (radiating burning or tingling into the sole), Baxter's nerve entrapment, and heel fat pad atrophy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establish the diagnosis through pattern recognition — first-step morning pain, point tenderness over the medial calcaneal tubercle, positive windlass test, and limited ankle dorsiflexion together reach a positive predictive value over 90% in primary care.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Your clinician asks when the pain started, what makes it worse, and presses different spots on the heel and arch. They check ankle and toe range of motion, watch you walk, and may ask you to stand on tiptoe. Visit time 15-20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reproduces plantar fascia pain by passively extending the great toe with the ankle in neutral, tensioning the fascia. A positive test supports the diagnosis with roughly 32% sensitivity and 100% specificity (De Garceau 2003).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician sits you on the exam table, holds your forefoot, and pulls the big toe upward. Sharp pain at the heel insertion under this stretch is a positive result. The test takes 30 seconds.

diagnosticTests.test: Diagnostic ultrasound of the plantar fascia
diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures fascia thickness at the calcaneal insertion (normal under 4 mm) and detects partial tears, perifascial fluid, or paratenon thickening. Used when symptoms persist beyond 3 months, when rupture is suspected, or to guide injection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician applies gel and a probe under the heel and arch while you lie prone with feet hanging off the bed. Painless, 10-15 minutes, no radiation. Most results are explained in real time.

diagnosticTests.test: Plain radiograph (weight-bearing lateral and axial heel views)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out calcaneal stress fracture, calcaneal cysts, bone tumors, and severe arthritic change. Heel spurs seen on plain film are incidental in most cases and not the source of pain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two or three quick X-ray views with the foot bearing weight where possible. Total time under 10 minutes; results often available within an hour.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for refractory cases, suspected partial or complete fascia rupture, suspected calcaneal stress fracture, or pre-surgical planning. Shows fascia thickening, marrow edema, and accurately distinguishes stress fracture from fasciopathy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Lying still inside the scanner for 25-40 minutes with the affected foot in a coil. No injection unless gadolinium contrast is requested for a specific question. Results read by a musculoskeletal radiologist within 24-72 hours in most centers.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when neurologic symptoms (burning, tingling, numbness in the sole or toes) raise suspicion of tarsal tunnel syndrome, Baxter's nerve entrapment, or S1 radiculopathy rather than isolated plantar fasciitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Surface electrodes record nerve responses to small electrical stimuli; a fine needle may sample muscle electrical activity. Mildly uncomfortable but tolerable. Takes 30-60 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Considered when both heels are affected, when patients are under 40, when other entheses are inflamed, or when there is morning stiffness or psoriasis. Includes ESR, CRP, HLA-B27, rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP, and uric acid.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw at a lab or clinic. Results return in 1-5 days depending on the panel. Most plantar fasciitis patients do not need this testing.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of plantar fasciitis follows a tiered, time-based approach endorsed by the 2014 APTA clinical practice guideline (Martin et al., updated 2023), ACFAS clinical consensus, and current sports medicine literature. The first tier, delivered in the first 6-12 weeks, is education plus mechanical load reduction: relative rest from aggravating activity, supportive cushioned footwear, prefabricated arch supports, daily plantar fascia-specific stretching (DiGiovanni 2003/2006), gastrocnemius-soleus stretching, and ice or contrast bathing. Around 70% of patients improve substantially within this window. Tier two adds physical therapy with manual therapy and progressive calf and intrinsic foot strengthening, dorsiflexion night splints for patients with morning pain, custom orthoses for those with significant pes planus or cavus, and short courses of oral NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily for 7-14 days, or naproxen 500 mg twice daily) for symptom control. Corticosteroid injection (methylprednisolone 40 mg or triamcinolone 40 mg) can break a stubborn pain cycle but provides only short-term benefit (4-12 weeks) and carries a small but real risk of fat pad atrophy and fascia rupture; ultrasound guidance reduces both risks. Tier three, for patients still symptomatic at 6 months, includes extracorporeal shockwave therapy — both radial and focused — which produces durable pain reduction in randomized trials with number needed to treat around 3-4 (Babatunde 2019 network meta-analysis). Platelet-rich plasma injection and ultrasound-guided percutaneous needle tenotomy are reasonable evidence-supported alternatives. Surgery — partial plantar fasciotomy, gastrocnemius recession, or both — is reserved for the roughly 5-10% of patients who fail all conservative measures at 12 months. Recovery from open or endoscopic fasciotomy takes 3-6 months; gastrocnemius recession often produces faster pain relief with lower risk of arch collapse. Patients should expect a gradual reduction in pain rather than overnight resolution; the natural history of plantar fasciitis is recovery in 90% by 12 months even without intervention.

medicalTreatments.name: Plantar fascia-specific stretching (DiGiovanni protocol)
medicalTreatments.description: Three sets of 10 stretches, three times daily, performed seated with the affected leg crossed over the other and the toes pulled into dorsiflexion to tension the fascia. Performed for at least 8 weeks. Targets the fascia directly rather than the calf alone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain relief in roughly 75% of patients at 8 weeks and durable benefit at 2-year follow-up versus Achilles stretching alone (DiGiovanni 2006 RCT).

medicalTreatments.name: Gastrocnemius-soleus stretching and eccentric heel drops
medicalTreatments.description: Daily standing calf stretches (gastrocnemius with the knee extended, soleus with the knee bent), held 30 seconds and repeated 3-5 times, paired with eccentric heel drops off a step. Addresses the dorsiflexion limitation that drives fascia overload.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces pain by 40-60% over 6-12 weeks in randomized trials; less effective as monotherapy than when combined with fascia-specific stretching.

medicalTreatments.name: Prefabricated or custom foot orthoses
medicalTreatments.description: Off-the-shelf arch supports (e.g., Powerstep, Superfeet) work as well as custom orthoses for most patients in randomized trials; custom devices are reserved for severe pes planus, cavus, or persistent symptoms after 3 months of prefabricated trial.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Both prefabricated and custom orthoses reduce pain by approximately 30-40% at 3 months versus sham (Landorf 2006); benefits plateau after 12 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Dorsiflexion night splint
medicalTreatments.description: Holds the ankle at 90 degrees or slight dorsiflexion overnight to prevent the fascia from shortening during sleep. Reduces the severity of first-step morning pain. Front-loading splints are typically better tolerated than posterior versions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction in 55-80% of patients with morning-dominant symptoms at 8 weeks (Powell 1998); benefit greatest in patients with symptoms over 6 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily; naproxen 500 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Short courses (7-14 days) provide analgesia and modest tissue effect during acute flares. Use the lowest effective dose with food; avoid in CKD, anticoagulation, peptic ulcer disease, and cardiovascular disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain relief in roughly 50-70% of patients at 2 weeks; effect is symptomatic and does not change the underlying degenerative process.

medicalTreatments.name: Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection (methylprednisolone 40 mg or triamcinolone 40 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Single injection placed deep to the plantar fascia at the calcaneal insertion under ultrasound guidance. Reserved for patients who fail 6-8 weeks of stretching and orthoses. Limited to one or two injections to reduce fat pad atrophy and rupture risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 60-70% at 4 weeks but only 30% at 6 months versus placebo (Crawford 1999); rupture risk approximately 2-10% with repeat injections.

medicalTreatments.name: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT)
medicalTreatments.description: Radial or focused shockwaves delivered in 3-5 weekly sessions to the plantar fascia origin. Stimulates neovascularization and fibroblast activity. Standard option for symptoms over 6 months that have failed first-line treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction in 60-80% of patients at 12 weeks with number needed to treat around 3-4 (Babatunde 2019 network meta-analysis of 30 RCTs); benefits persist at 12 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma injection
medicalTreatments.description: Autologous PRP prepared from a venous blood draw and injected under ultrasound guidance into the proximal plantar fascia. Effects are slower and more durable than corticosteroid, with no fat pad or rupture risk. Coverage and cost vary widely.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction comparable to corticosteroid at 4 weeks but superior at 6 and 12 months in meta-analyses (Hurley 2020); about 60-70% of patients improve.

medicalTreatments.name: Ultrasound-guided percutaneous needle tenotomy or Tenex
medicalTreatments.description: Needle fenestration or Tenex (ultrasonic tissue debridement) of the degenerative fascia under ultrasound guidance. Brief office procedure under local anesthetic. Reserved for chronic refractory cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improvement in roughly 65-80% of patients at 6 months in cohort studies; randomized evidence is still emerging.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic or open partial plantar fasciotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Partial release of the medial third of the plantar fascia at the calcaneal insertion through a small incision or arthroscope. Reserved for patients with persistent disabling pain after 12 months of structured conservative care.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient-reported good or excellent outcomes in 70-90% of selected patients; risk of arch collapse and lateral column pain in 10-15% if excessive fascia is released.

surgicalOptions.name: Gastrocnemius recession (Strayer or Vulpius procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: Lengthening of the gastrocnemius aponeurosis in the proximal calf to correct equinus contracture that loads the fascia. Increasingly favored over fasciotomy because it preserves the plantar fascia and arch support.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain reduction in 80-90% of carefully selected patients at 12 months (Maskill 2010; Molund 2018); lower risk of arch collapse than fasciotomy.

surgicalOptions.name: Combined gastrocnemius recession plus partial fasciotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for severe combined equinus and refractory plantar fasciitis. Addresses both the proximal calf contracture and the local fascia degeneration in one operation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient satisfaction near 85% in small case series; recovery takes 3-4 months before return to full activity.

alternativeTreatments.name: Trigger-point dry needling
alternativeTreatments.description: Cotchett 2014 randomized controlled trial showed that dry needling of myofascial trigger points in the calf and intrinsic foot muscles produced clinically meaningful pain reduction at 6 weeks versus sham. Reasonable adjunct delivered by trained physiotherapists.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-Dye taping
alternativeTreatments.description: Strapping technique that offloads the plantar fascia and supports the medial arch. Reduces pain during weight-bearing in randomized trials (Hyland 2006); useful as a short-term bridge while stretching and orthoses take effect.

alternativeTreatments.name: Myofascial release and instrument-assisted soft tissue mobilization
alternativeTreatments.description: Foam roller or massage stick work along the calf and the sole of the foot improves dorsiflexion and reduces tenderness in small trials. Best used in combination with stretching rather than as a stand-alone.

whySeeSpecialist: See a podiatrist or sports medicine physician if heel pain has not improved after 6-8 weeks of supportive footwear, stretching, and load reduction; if the diagnosis is unclear because of bilateral pain, radiating burning, or pain at rest; if you suspect a plantar fascia rupture after a popping injury; or if you are considering injection, shockwave, or surgery. Most uncomplicated cases improve in primary care with a structured stretching and footwear program.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a podiatrist board-certified in foot and ankle surgery or a sports medicine physician with regular use of musculoskeletal ultrasound. Ask whether the practice offers diagnostic ultrasound, image-guided injection, and shockwave therapy in-house, and whether they routinely work with a physical therapist who delivers DiGiovanni-style fascia-specific stretching. Continuity matters — most plantar fasciitis treatment unfolds over 3-12 months across several visits.
hospitalCriteria: Podiatry or foot and ankle orthopedic clinic with on-site musculoskeletal ultrasound
Access to a physical therapist trained in plantar fascia-specific stretching and graded loading
Availability of extracorporeal shockwave therapy (radial or focused) for chronic cases
Capacity for ultrasound-guided injection (corticosteroid or PRP) when indicated
keyFacilities: Tertiary foot and ankle centers within academic orthopedic departments
Sports medicine clinics with integrated physiotherapy and shockwave services
Private podiatry practices offering custom orthotic fabrication and biomechanical gait analysis
preventionStrategies: Build mileage and standing time gradually — increase running volume or new occupational standing by no more than 10-15% per week to give the fascia time to adapt
Stretch the calf and plantar fascia daily, especially before getting out of bed and after long sitting periods, if you have a history of heel pain or limited ankle dorsiflexion
Replace running shoes every 500-800 km and choose work shoes with a cushioned midsole and supportive arch if you stand more than 4 hours daily
Maintain body weight in the normal range (BMI under 30) — weight reduction of 5-10% measurably reduces both incidence and recurrence risk
Use anti-fatigue mats and rotate between two pairs of supportive shoes if your job requires prolonged standing on hard floors
Address foot architecture early with prefabricated arch supports in flat or high-arched feet, particularly if you have already had one episode
lifestyleModifications: Switch from barefoot, ballet flat, or worn footwear to cushioned, supportive shoes with a 5-10 mm heel-to-toe drop for daily wear during recovery
Avoid going barefoot at home — wear supportive house shoes or sandals with arch support such as Birkenstocks or Vionic
Reduce or temporarily stop high-impact activity (running, jumping, hill walking) for 4-8 weeks and substitute cycling, swimming, or elliptical for cardiovascular fitness
Apply ice or roll a frozen water bottle under the arch for 10-15 minutes after activity to reduce post-exertion pain
Lose excess weight gradually through diet and low-impact exercise — even a 5% reduction in body weight measurably reduces fascia loading
Use a dorsiflexion night splint if morning pain is severe; sock-style splints (Strassburg) are usually better tolerated than rigid posterior versions
Track flares in a simple diary noting footwear, surface, and activity — patterns often reveal preventable triggers

recommended: Mediterranean-style eating pattern with vegetables, whole grains, fish, and olive oil — anti-inflammatory effects support general musculoskeletal recovery
Adequate protein (1.0-1.2 g/kg body weight daily) to support collagen turnover during rehabilitation
Vitamin D-rich foods or supplementation in patients with documented deficiency, given the role of vitamin D in musculoskeletal pain
Fatty fish or omega-3 supplementation (1-2 g daily) for modest anti-inflammatory benefit
Plenty of water — dehydration impairs connective tissue elasticity
avoid: Excess ultra-processed food and sugar-sweetened beverages — both contribute to weight gain that loads the fascia
Alcohol in heavy or daily amounts — interferes with sleep, recovery, and weight management
Crash diets or extreme calorie restriction — protein deficiency slows connective tissue repair
Reliance on energy drinks or excessive caffeine as a substitute for sleep, which is when most tissue repair occurs

exerciseGuidelines: During acute symptoms (first 2-6 weeks), reduce or stop weight-bearing impact activity and substitute cycling, swimming, or aqua-jogging for cardio. Continue daily plantar fascia-specific stretching and calf stretching. Once first-step morning pain falls below 3 out of 10, gradually reintroduce walking, then easy running on softer surfaces, increasing volume by no more than 10% weekly. Eccentric heel drops, intrinsic foot strengthening (towel scrunches, toe yoga), and single-leg balance work progress over 8-12 weeks. Avoid hill running, sprints, and barefoot training until pain has been absent for at least 4 weeks.
dailyManagement: Perform plantar fascia-specific stretching first thing in the morning before standing — three sets of ten repetitions with the toes pulled into dorsiflexion
Wear supportive footwear from the moment you get out of bed; keep cushioned slippers or sandals next to the bed
Roll a frozen water bottle or chilled massage ball under the arch for 10-15 minutes after work or exercise
Take prescribed analgesics with food and only for as long as needed; reassess at 1-2 weeks
Track pain on a 0-10 scale at first morning step and at the end of the day to monitor trajectory across weeks rather than days
prognosis: Around 80-90% of patients with plantar fasciitis recover fully within 6-12 months with conservative treatment, and roughly 90% recover by 18-24 months whether or not they undertake formal therapy. Adherence to fascia-specific stretching, supportive footwear, and load management are the strongest modifiable predictors of faster recovery. Negative prognostic factors include obesity, bilateral disease, symptoms over 6 months at first specialist visit, occupational standing more than 6 hours daily, and severe limitation of ankle dorsiflexion. About 10% of patients develop chronic, recalcitrant symptoms that persist beyond 12 months; most of these respond to extracorporeal shockwave therapy, PRP, or surgery. Recurrence after successful treatment occurs in around 15-20% of patients over 5 years, usually triggered by a return to overload, weight gain, or unsupportive footwear. Long-term, plantar fasciitis does not cause permanent disability, joint destruction, or systemic complications when managed appropriately, and the great majority of patients return to their preferred activities.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients notice meaningful improvement in first-step morning pain within 6-12 weeks of starting a structured stretching, footwear, and load-reduction program. Full symptom resolution typically occurs between 6 and 12 months. Patients receiving extracorporeal shockwave therapy usually report improvement 4-12 weeks after the treatment course. Recovery from corticosteroid injection is fastest in the short term but tends to plateau by 12 weeks. After surgical fasciotomy or gastrocnemius recession, expect 6-12 weeks before walking comfortably and 3-6 months before return to running or court sports.
complications: Plantar fascia rupture — sudden tearing of a weakened fascia, often after corticosteroid injection or during sprinting, with bruising, swelling, and inability to push off; occurs in 2-10% of patients receiving repeated steroid injections
Heel fat pad atrophy from repeated corticosteroid injection, leading to thin, painful heel padding and chronic plantar heel pain that is harder to treat than the original condition
Chronic, recalcitrant plantar fasciitis lasting beyond 12 months in approximately 10% of patients, requiring escalation to shockwave, PRP, or surgery
Altered gait and secondary lower-limb pain — chronic limping shifts load and can produce knee, hip, or contralateral foot pain over months
Arch collapse and lateral column overload after excessive surgical fasciotomy, manifesting as midfoot pain and pes planus
Posterior tibial tendinopathy and Achilles tendinopathy as secondary overload conditions in patients who limp or alter their gait for many months

supportResources.name: American Orthopaedic Foot & Ankle Society — Plantar Fasciitis Patient Information
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the orthopedic foot and ankle specialty society.

supportResources.name: American Podiatric Medical Association — Heel Pain
supportResources.description: Patient guide from the US podiatric medical association covering symptoms, treatment, and prevention.

supportResources.name: NHS — Heel pain (plantar fasciitis)
supportResources.description: UK National Health Service patient guide with self-care advice and when to seek help.

confusedWithConditions.name: Heel Spur (Calcaneal Spur)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A heel spur is a bony outgrowth from the calcaneus seen on X-ray that is often associated with plantar fasciitis but is not the cause of pain. Around 15-20% of asymptomatic adults have an incidental spur. Pain comes from the fascia, not the bone; removing the spur surgically is no longer routinely recommended.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tarsal tunnel syndrome causes burning, tingling, or numbness radiating into the sole and toes from posterior tibial nerve compression behind the medial ankle. The pain is neuropathic and often worse at night without first-step pattern. Tinel's sign behind the medial malleolus and nerve conduction studies distinguish it.

confusedWithConditions.name: Achilles Tendinitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Achilles tendinitis produces pain and tenderness 2-6 cm above the heel along the tendon, worst with toe-off and after activity, not under the heel itself with first morning steps. The two conditions can coexist because both involve the calf-fascia chain.

confusedWithConditions.name: Calcaneal Stress Fracture
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A calcaneal stress fracture causes deep, diffuse heel pain that is present at rest and worsens with any weight-bearing, with a positive heel squeeze test. Common in runners and military recruits after training spikes. MRI confirms; bone rest is required for 6-12 weeks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Metatarsalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Metatarsalgia produces pain under the ball of the foot, not the heel, worst with push-off and prolonged standing. The pain is over the metatarsal heads rather than the medial calcaneal tubercle, and stretching the great toe does not reproduce it.

relatedConditions.name: Heel Spur
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently coexists with plantar fasciitis and visible on the same X-rays, but is rarely the actual source of pain.

relatedConditions.name: Achilles Tendinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the gastrocnemius-soleus contracture pathway with plantar fasciitis; both can present together in runners.

relatedConditions.name: Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Important neurologic mimic of medial heel pain that must be excluded when burning or tingling is present.

relatedConditions.name: Metatarsalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Common secondary condition when patients shift weight forward to offload a painful heel for an extended period.

relatedConditions.name: Morton's Neuroma
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common forefoot pain syndrome encountered in patients with altered gait or unsupportive footwear; often discussed in the same podiatry visit.

relatedConditions.name: Stress Fracture
relatedConditions.relevance: Calcaneal stress fracture is the most important imaging-confirmed differential in runners and military recruits with deep heel pain.

faqs.question: What is plantar fasciitis in plain language?
faqs.answer: Plantar fasciitis is degeneration and small tears of the thick band of tissue (the plantar fascia) that runs along the bottom of your foot from the heel to the toes. It usually causes sharp pain under the inside of the heel that is worst with the first few steps in the morning and after long periods of sitting. Despite the name ending in -itis, biopsy studies show it is a wear-and-tear injury rather than classic inflammation.

faqs.question: Why is plantar fasciitis pain worst in the morning?
faqs.answer: Overnight, the fascia shortens and partially heals microtears in a contracted position. When you step out of bed, the sudden stretch reopens these tiny tears and reproduces the original pain. After 5-15 minutes of walking, the fascia warms and loosens, and the pain eases. The same pattern returns after any long sitting period.

faqs.question: How long does plantar fasciitis take to heal?
faqs.answer: Roughly 80-90% of patients fully recover within 6-12 months with stretching, supportive footwear, and reduced impact activity. About 90% recover by 18-24 months regardless of treatment. Around 10% of patients have symptoms longer than a year and benefit from shockwave therapy, PRP, or surgery.

faqs.question: What are the best shoes for plantar fasciitis?
faqs.answer: Choose shoes with a cushioned midsole, firm heel counter, supportive arch, and a 5-10 mm heel-to-toe drop. Avoid completely flat shoes, ballet flats, and worn-out trainers. Replace running shoes every 500-800 km. Brands frequently recommended by podiatrists include Hoka, Brooks, Asics, New Balance, and Vionic; orthotic-friendly sandals such as Birkenstocks work well indoors.

faqs.question: Should I keep running if I have plantar fasciitis?
faqs.answer: Reduce or stop running for 2-6 weeks while pain is most severe, and switch to cycling, swimming, or elliptical to maintain fitness. Once first-step morning pain falls below 3 out of 10, return gradually with shorter runs on softer surfaces, increasing weekly mileage by no more than 10%. Continuing high mileage through significant pain prolongs the condition.

faqs.question: What stretches help plantar fasciitis the most?
faqs.answer: The strongest evidence supports plantar fascia-specific stretching (DiGiovanni protocol): sit with the affected leg crossed over the other, pull the toes back toward the shin until you feel a stretch along the arch, hold for 10 seconds, repeat 10 times, three times per day. Combine with standing calf stretches against a wall, holding 30 seconds and repeating 3-5 times.

faqs.question: Do I need an X-ray or MRI for plantar fasciitis?
faqs.answer: Most patients do not. The diagnosis is made on history and physical examination. Imaging is reserved for atypical features — bilateral pain in a young adult, symptoms over 3-6 months without improvement, suspected fascia rupture or calcaneal stress fracture, or pre-surgical planning. Ultrasound is the preferred first imaging test when needed; MRI is used for tougher cases.

faqs.question: Is plantar fasciitis the same as a heel spur?
faqs.answer: No. A heel spur is a bony outgrowth from the calcaneus visible on X-ray, while plantar fasciitis is degeneration of the fascia itself. Around 15-20% of people without any heel pain have a heel spur as an incidental finding. Removing the spur surgically is no longer routinely recommended because the spur is not the source of pain.

faqs.question: Do steroid injections work for plantar fasciitis?
faqs.answer: A single corticosteroid injection placed under ultrasound guidance can reduce pain by 60-70% at 4 weeks, but the benefit fades by 3-6 months in most patients. Repeat injections raise the risk of heel fat pad atrophy and fascia rupture (2-10% with multiple injections), so most specialists limit treatment to one or two injections at intervals of at least 3 months.

faqs.question: Does shockwave therapy actually work?
faqs.answer: Yes. A 2019 network meta-analysis of 30 randomized trials (Babatunde 2019) showed that both radial and focused extracorporeal shockwave therapy reduce pain in 60-80% of patients with chronic plantar fasciitis at 12 weeks, with benefits persisting at 1 year. Number needed to treat is roughly 3-4. It is most useful for symptoms lasting longer than 6 months.

faqs.question: Should I wear a night splint for plantar fasciitis?
faqs.answer: Night splints are most helpful for patients with severe first-step morning pain or symptoms lasting longer than 6 weeks. They hold the ankle at 90 degrees overnight to prevent the fascia from shortening during sleep. About 55-80% of patients improve. Front-loading sock-style splints (Strassburg) tend to be better tolerated than rigid posterior versions.

faqs.question: Are custom orthotics better than over-the-counter inserts?
faqs.answer: For most patients with plantar fasciitis, prefabricated arch supports work as well as custom orthotics in randomized trials at 12 weeks. Custom devices are reserved for severe flat feet, very high arches, leg-length differences, or patients who fail a 3-month trial of prefabricated supports. Start with a quality off-the-shelf option such as Powerstep or Superfeet.

faqs.question: Can losing weight help my plantar fasciitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Higher body mass index is one of the most consistent risk factors for plantar fasciitis and for slower recovery. BMI above 30 raises risk roughly 5-6 fold. Even a 5-10% weight reduction measurably reduces fascia loading and improves both incidence and persistence in cohort studies. Pair gradual weight loss with low-impact exercise such as cycling or swimming.

faqs.question: Is plantar fasciitis a sign of something more serious?
faqs.answer: Most cases are isolated overuse injuries. However, bilateral plantar fasciitis in a young adult, especially with morning back stiffness, psoriasis, or other joint pain, can be the first sign of seronegative spondyloarthritis (ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis). Diabetes is also associated with thicker, slower-healing fascia. Tell your clinician if symptoms are bilateral or accompanied by systemic features.

faqs.question: Can plantar fasciitis affect both feet at once?
faqs.answer: Yes. Around 20-30% of patients have bilateral disease, often related to occupational standing, obesity, or systemic enthesopathy. Bilateral symptoms in a younger patient should prompt screening for inflammatory causes such as ankylosing spondylitis or psoriatic arthritis. Treatment principles are the same as for unilateral disease, but recovery is generally slower.

faqs.question: Will I need surgery for plantar fasciitis?
faqs.answer: Probably not. Only about 5-10% of patients ultimately need surgery, and only after 9-12 months of structured conservative treatment have failed. Modern surgical options include partial plantar fasciotomy and gastrocnemius recession; the latter preserves the fascia and is increasingly preferred. Good-to-excellent outcomes occur in 70-90% of carefully selected patients.

faqs.question: Can plantar fasciitis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Recurrence occurs in roughly 15-20% of patients over 5 years, usually after a return to overload (training spikes, new standing job, weight gain) or a return to unsupportive footwear. Continuing daily calf and fascia stretching, sensible footwear, and gradual training increases reduces the risk substantially.

faqs.question: How much does plantar fasciitis treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Most first-line treatment is inexpensive: prefabricated arch supports cost USD 30-60, generic NSAIDs and night splints cost USD 20-50, and physical therapy is typically covered by insurance with copays of USD 20-50 per visit. Extracorporeal shockwave courses run USD 500-2,000 out of pocket where insurance does not cover it. Surgical fasciotomy or gastrocnemius recession ranges from USD 3,000-8,000 with insurance and considerably more without.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for heel pain?
faqs.answer: See a clinician if heel pain has not improved after 2-4 weeks of supportive footwear, stretching, and reduced activity; if the pain is severe enough to limit walking; if you suspect a popping injury and possible fascia rupture; if you have numbness or tingling in the sole; or if both heels are painful in a person under 40. Urgent care is needed for fever, redness, or sudden swelling.

faqs.question: Can plantar fasciitis cause back, knee, or hip pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Limping to offload a painful heel for weeks or months shifts load to the opposite foot, knee, hip, and lower back. Around a quarter of long-standing cases develop secondary knee or hip discomfort. Treating the underlying heel pain promptly and avoiding prolonged altered gait reduces this risk.

faqs.question: Does walking barefoot make plantar fasciitis worse?
faqs.answer: For most people with active plantar fasciitis, yes. Walking barefoot on hard surfaces (tile, hardwood, concrete) removes arch support and cushioning and worsens fascia loading. Switch to supportive house shoes or arch-supported sandals indoors. Once symptoms have resolved for several months, occasional barefoot walking on soft surfaces such as grass or sand is fine and may strengthen intrinsic foot muscles.

symptomKeywords: pain first step out of bed
metaDescription: Plantar fasciitis: sharp first-step heel pain from fascia overload. Symptoms, stretches, shockwave, surgery & recovery reviewed by our medical board.
